items.rationale: Seen as crypto's first clear product-market fit and 'killer app' — cheapest way to move dollars, sub-penny L2 transfers, the on-ramp to bring billions onchain and a USD-extending answer to CBDCs.
items.age_note: 2023 view framed stablecoins mainly as crypto-internal leverage/store-of-value; by 2024-2025 elevated to proven 'killer app / PMF' — conviction strengthened over time

citations.quote: Stablecoins are the "killer app" of crypto, and here's a bunch of great data that shows it.
Stablecoin payments could be the answer to how we bring billions of people onchain.
Stablecoin payments and AI agents are some of the clearest use cases for crypto since Bitcoin's store of value.

items.rationale: Viewed as the endgame for blockchain scalability and a new developer primitive ('verifiable compute'); Jolt democratizes SNARKs without circuit expertise and falling prover overhead keeps expanding the application surface.

citations.quote: I still think ZK is under hyped.
Fastest zkVM in the world. Best developer experience. 100% open source and a public good.
as you bring that Pro overhead down the number of applicable applications for snarks grows dramatically

items.rationale: Framed as the path to consumer crypto and the 'native antidote to physical infrastructure monopolies' — user-owned/operated physical services that centralized networks can't replicate; energy especially (Daylight investment).
items.age_note: definition actively narrowed through 2024 (excludes Bitcoin mining, decentralized cloud); conviction steady

citations.quote: The future of consumer crypto? DePIN
the same can be said for DePIN networks being the native antidote to physical infrastructure monopolies.
DePIN offers the opportunity for the physical world to be dominated by user generated services.

items.rationale: Blockchains seen as the counterbalance to centralized Big-AI: enforcing ownership, verifying identity/authenticity against deepfakes, decentralizing compute, and giving AI agents wallets. 'AI needs blockchain-enabled computing.'

citations.quote: AI needs blockchain-enabled computing.
AI agents can't have bank accounts, but they can have crypto wallets.
Crypto can carry the torch of the open internet and help keep it creative, open, and diverse.

items.rationale: Rollup-centric roadmap plus EigenLayer (restaking as 'verifiable cloud'), shared sequencing (Espresso), and real-time proofs to unify fragmented rollups; infra is now 'ready for apps to flourish.'
items.age_note: enthusiasm explicitly tempered by liquidity-fragmentation concern (see concerned list)

citations.quote: EigenLayer has the potential to unlock 100x faster innovation
Ethereum cannot afford to slow down.

items.rationale: A programmable onchain IP layer is seen as essential infrastructure for the AI-content explosion (attribution/licensing); NFTs enable creator royalties, digital property rights and low-cost collectibles.

citations.quote: I don't think I've ever seen an infrastructure innovation that excites me as much as @StoryProtocol.
We desperately need an open, global coordination layer for IP

items.rationale: Popular and growing; thesis that DeFi needs UX simplification to reach next 100M users (Legend) and cross-chain asset access (Universal), plus more expressive ZK-proven pricing/risk.

citations.quote: DeFi remains popular — and it's growing
to simplify DeFi and help bring the next 100M users onchain.

items.rationale: Privacy deemed prerequisite for mainstream adoption; web proofs/zkTLS seen as critical to onboard a billion users and import exogenous incentives; coSNARKs enable private shared state.
items.age_note: enthusiasm now paired with sharp 'trusted ≠ trustless' caution on TEEs (Feb 2025)

citations.quote: I'm now convinced that web proofs/zkTLS will play a critical role in getting us there.
It's hard to imagine crypto ever reaching mainstream adoption without privacy.

items.rationale: Legal wrappers (DUNA) now a 'strategic imperative' after adverse general-partnership rulings; optimism about liquid-democracy/delegation experiments and trust-minimized governance.
items.age_note: shifted from research-curiosity (2023) to urgent practical necessity after Nov 2024 California DAO liability ruling

citations.quote: It's now a strategic imperative for DAO's to use legal wrappers.

items.rationale: Considered the primary force holding the space back: deters quality founders, arms politicized regulators, distorts how the public sees crypto, and substitutes value-extraction for value-creation. Technically uninteresting and zero-sum.
items.age_note: remarkably consistent from Dec 2023 through Feb 2025; never softened

citations.quote: We don't need the casino. We never did. It's the primary force holding the space back.
memecoins aren't very technically interesting.
Memecoins are the opposite of equity — ownership of nothing versus something.

items.rationale: Enables insiders to dump on retail, fuels pump-and-dumps, misaligns incentives; unfettered early secondary liquidity seen as an 'existential threat to innovation.' Advocates 4yr/1yr-cliff standard and decentralization-milestone unlocks.

citations.quote: 4 year/1 year cliup token lockups should be standard.
Unfettered access to secondary market liquidity is an existential threat to innovation.

items.rationale: Regulation-by-enforcement bred uncertainty, drove builders offshore, and was used as 'lawfare' against decentralized tech — viewed as a national-security risk for US crypto leadership.
items.age_note: ACUTE through 2023-2024; markedly cooled by Feb 2025 — SEC dropping/settling cases, Coinbase case dismissed, 'weaponization is at an end,' optimism for legislation in 2025

citations.quote: regulation-by-enforcement is no substitute for formal rulemaking and public comment processes
The weaponization of regulatory uncertainty by the SEC is at an end.

items.rationale: PBS decentralizes validators but centralizes builders via a 'proof-of-MEV' feedback loop (exclusive order flow → bigger get bigger), risking censorship; MEV remains a tax/'problem for users.' Multi-block/cross-chain MEV makes it worse.

citations.quote: PBS is reducing centralization in validation, but might create centralization in building
Mev remains what I would consider a problem for users users are losing a lot of money

items.rationale: Hand-written circuits are 'riddled with bugs'; today's deployed SNARKs are protected by obscurity, not their security property. Seen as the biggest threat to SNARKs fulfilling their potential — bigger than performance.

citations.quote: the security issues cannot be overstated I think they are the biggest threat to snarks not fulfilling their potential

items.rationale: The rollup-centric future fragments Ethereum's composability and liquidity; cross-rollup interop, shared sequencing and real-time proofs are needed before vertically-integrated L1s capture the liquidity.

citations.quote: fragmented liquidity and fractured state present a challenge and an opportunity.

items.rationale: Binary collapse risk and death spirals (Terra); flight-to-safety drains liquidity precisely when most needed, imposing run-risk externalities; regulatory consensus against algo designs. 'No LUNA please.'
items.age_note: core analysis from 2023 research; lightly restated Feb 2025 — concern persists alongside excitement for non-fiat experiments like RAI

citations.quote: consensus among Regulators in the U.S and Europe... against algorithmic stable coins
I expect more experiments here (no LUNA please).

items.rationale: Growing reliance on trusted execution environments as if equivalent to trustless guarantees; 'defense in depth' use is fine but conflation is expected to cause 'a lot of pain.'

citations.quote: The conflation of trusted execution environments with trustless blockchains is going to lead to a lot of pain.

items.rationale: a16z portfolio companies (mostly pre-revenue, pre-token, ordinary expenses) lost banking access without justification — viewed as an unjustified, escalating systemic attack on the industry.
items.age_note: acute late-2024 issue; expected to evolve with the new administration

citations.quote: Most debankings of @a16zcrypto portcos happened to pre-revenue and pre-token companies.

items.rationale: Base settlement/security layer for rollups and restaking; expected to be deflationary long-term; formally recognized as not-a-security (commodity), the template for a legitimate decentralized 'network token.'

citations.quote: long term I think most of us expect eth to be a deflationary currency
ETH is not a security, nor should transactions in ETH be securities transactions.

items.rationale: Digital gold and most secure, decentralized network; economic characteristics 'unlike any memecoin.' Excited about the asset, though skeptical of prioritizing a US strategic reserve over building.
items.age_note: store-of-value thesis steady; Nov 2024 added caveat that strategic-reserve push is a misranked priority

citations.quote: being the native asset of what is arguably the most secure and decentralized network gives it economic characteristics... unlike any memecoin
digital gold—a store of value, albeit a volatile one.

items.rationale: Transparent, safe-haven stablecoin (flight-to-safety asset in stress); sub-penny L2 transfers driving the killer-app thesis; viewed as 'likely okay' under securities law, treated like digital cash.

citations.quote: USDC transfers on Ethereum L2s now cost less than a penny.
USDC is likely okay

items.rationale: Leads on usage metrics — top chain by monthly active addresses and a major share of DEX/low-fee activity (with Base). Mostly data-driven recognition rather than a thesis bet; sandwiching/validator behavior noted as a negative.
items.age_note: inferred from usage data cited approvingly; no explicit token endorsement

citations.quote: The top chain by monthly active addresses: @solana.

items.rationale: No intrinsic value, no network, no value creation — 'ownership of nothing'; the crypto equivalent of chain emails / slot machines. The line between memecoin and scamcoin is hard to discern.

citations.quote: Memecoins are the crypto equivalent of chain emails.
True memecoins have no intrinsic value (no utility, no company, no anything)

items.rationale: Less transparent reserves; in past stress events markets fled toward more transparent coins amid concerns about Tether's ability to maintain the peg. Used as a regulatory-arbitrage refuge but with quality-of-reserves doubts.
items.age_note: based on 2023 research; not restated recently, but no contrary statement

citations.quote: concerns about the quality of the assets of tether and which also was particularly not transparent

items.rationale: Tokens tied to a single controlling company (vs. a decentralized network) carry securities-like risk profiles and information asymmetries — 'just a security' (FTT-style). The new 'meta' of launching company tokens or tokenized securities while ignoring securities law is seen as dangerous.
items.age_note: intensified Feb 2025 as company-tied token launches proliferated

citations.quote: If it's from a centralized business, like FTT, then it's just a security.
centralized companies launching tokens that are directly tied to their business and ongoing operations are pretty bad facts

items.rationale: Reserves from self-created tokens lead to death spirals when market belief breaks; cautionary archetype for stablecoin design.
items.age_note: Terra collapse analysis from 2023; echoed Feb 2025 ('no LUNA please')

citations.quote: as we've seen in the context of Terra um these things could lead very quickly to death Spirals and unravel

risk_regime.rationale: As of Feb 2025 the house is decidedly constructive: SEC enforcement retreating (Coinbase case dropped), bipartisan/legislative momentum, infrastructure 'now ready for apps to flourish,' and usage metrics (mobile wallets, stablecoin volume, active users) at repeated all-time highs — 'there has never been a better time to start a project in crypto.' This risk-on posture is disciplined, not euphoric: paired with strong, persistent aversion to the memecoin casino, predatory token terms, and easy secondary liquidity, plus a security-first 'don't blow up' caution. Optimism is structural (build for the long term) rather than a call to chase the speculative cycle.

notes: Recency weighting drove three notable shifts: (1) Regulatory posture flipped from acute alarm (SEC 'lawfare,' offshoring, debanking through 2023-2024) to optimism by Feb 2025 ('weaponization is at an end'; legislative hope) — the underlying preference for clear rules and decentralization-as-control is unchanged, the threat assessment cooled. (2) Stablecoins were upgraded from a crypto-internal tool (2023) to the proven 'killer app' (2024-2025). (3) ZK enthusiasm now travels with an explicit security warning, and TEE/privacy excitement with a sharp 'trusted ≠ trustless' caveat. The corpus reflects multiple distinct voices — legal/policy (decentralization, Howey, token-design, lockups), cryptography research (Thaler on SNARKs/Jolt; MEV, restaking, stablecoin-run academic work), and thesis/infra (Dixon, Lazzarin, DePIN/AI/payments). The one near-unanimous, time-invariant view is the anti-speculation / pro-'real value-creation and decentralization' stance; it functions as the house's moral center across every topic. Token-level views are dominated by macro/structural and legal framing rather than price calls — a16z rarely makes tactical price bets in this corpus, so most token convictions reflect 'is this a legitimate, durable asset class' rather than near-term direction.
